title: When the Light Goes Out
synopsis: A tiny thorn fairy must help repair the lighthouse lantern before sunset leaves ships in darkness. Told by the ancient lighthouse lens itself, this tale follows the fairy's repeated journeys up the spiral stairs, each time discovering new clues about how the great light works.

pages.text: I have watched over these waters for one hundred years, grinding salt and wind into my crystal face. Today, something is wrong. My great light flickers and dims, just as the fishing boats turn toward home. The keeper's family has been gone three days, and only the wind knows when they will return. But listen—tiny wings beat against the storm. A thorn fairy circles my tower, his mossy-green skin dark against the evening sky. The fairy lands on my window ledge, his wings like dried leaves trembling in the gale.

illustration.description: Inside the lighthouse lantern room, showing the massive crystal lens. Thistle, a tiny thorn fairy with mossy-green skin, pointed ears, wings like dried leaves, and a cap made from a chestnut husk, perches on the window ledge. The view shows darkening seas and distant fishing boats. Golden age illustration, Arthur Rackham inspired, detailed pen work, watercolor washes, ornate fairy tale, timeless classic, decorative borders, enchanted scenes.
illustration.alt: A tiny fairy sits on the lighthouse window as the light flickers

pages.text: The fairy's pointed ears twitch at the sound of my failing light—tick, tick, stop. Tick, tick, stop. He flies down the spiral stairs, counting each iron step. One hundred and twelve steps to the bottom, where the great clockwork mechanism turns my beam across the water. But the fairy does not know how lighthouse gears work, how the weight must fall to turn the wheels. He sees only shadows and spinning metal. The sun sinks lower. Orange light spills across the waves like honey from a broken jar.

illustration.description: The spiral iron staircase winds down through the lighthouse interior. Thistle, a tiny thorn fairy with mossy-green skin, pointed ears, wings like dried leaves, and a cap made from a chestnut husk, flies past the mechanical clockwork gears and weights. Shadows dance on the curved walls. Golden age illustration, Arthur Rackham inspired, detailed pen work, watercolor washes, ornate fairy tale, timeless classic, decorative borders, enchanted scenes.
illustration.alt: A fairy explores the mechanical workings inside a lighthouse

pages.text: Up the stairs the fairy climbs again, this time stopping at the oil room. Here, the keeper stored whale oil and wicks, brass polish and cleaning cloths. The fairy's chestnut-husk cap tilts as he studies the empty oil reservoir. His prickly nature shows—he mutters fairy curses at the darkness creeping across the water. But wait. In the corner sits a can of oil, forgotten behind a coil of rope. The fairy's loyal heart beats faster. Perhaps there is hope yet. The sun touches the horizon now, a red coin balanced on the world's edge.

illustration.description: The lighthouse oil storage room with shelves of supplies and equipment. Thistle, a tiny thorn fairy with mossy-green skin, pointed ears, wings like dried leaves, and a cap made from a chestnut husk, hovers near a forgotten oil can behind coiled rope. Brass instruments gleam on wooden shelves. Golden age illustration, Arthur Rackham inspired, detailed pen work, watercolor washes, ornate fairy tale, timeless classic, decorative borders, enchanted scenes.
illustration.alt: A fairy discovers an oil can in the lighthouse storage room

pages.text: The oil can is too heavy for fairy wings, but clever minds find ways. The fairy pushes the can to the stairs, then tips it so the golden oil flows down each step like a slow waterfall. Up he flies again, following his liquid trail. At the lamp room, he sees what I see—the wick has burned too low, the flame too weak to shine through my crystal heart. Fairy hands, small but steady, work the wick higher. Drop by drop, oil seeps up the cloth threads. The mechanism below still needs winding, but first, there must be flame.

illustration.description: The top of the lighthouse where the great lamp sits within the crystal lens housing. Thistle, a tiny thorn fairy with mossy-green skin, pointed ears, wings like dried leaves, and a cap made from a chestnut husk, adjusts the lamp wick with his tiny hands. Oil glistens on the lighthouse steps behind him. Golden age illustration, Arthur Rackham inspired, detailed pen work, watercolor washes, ornate fairy tale, timeless classic, decorative borders, enchanted scenes.
illustration.alt: A fairy adjusts the lighthouse lamp wick as oil flows on the stairs

pages.text: Down the stairs one last time, the fairy finds the heavy weight that powers my turning. Fairy magic cannot move iron, but fairy wisdom can. He unties the keeper's boat rope from its cleat and fastens it to the weight. Over the pulley it goes, down through the window to dangle above the crashing waves. The weight drops, the gears turn, and my beam sweeps across the darkening sea. The flame grows bright, the light steady and true. Three fishing boats see my beacon and turn safe toward shore. The fairy perches on my frame, tired but proud, as stars emerge overhead. Some say the lighthouse keeps itself, but I know better. Magic lives in small wings and loyal hearts.

illustration.description: The lighthouse beam shines brilliantly across dark waters, illuminating three fishing boats heading safely to shore. Thistle, a tiny thorn fairy with mossy-green skin, pointed ears, wings like dried leaves, and a cap made from a chestnut husk, sits proudly on the lighthouse lens frame as stars twinkle in the night sky above. Golden age illustration, Arthur Rackham inspired, detailed pen work, watercolor washes, ornate fairy tale, timeless classic, decorative borders, enchanted scenes.
illustration.alt: A fairy rests as the lighthouse beam guides boats safely home under the stars

metadata.lessonFocus: understanding how mechanical systems work through observation and experimentation

coverPrompt.description: A towering white lighthouse stands at the edge of a windswept cliff, waves crashing below. Thistle, a tiny thorn fairy with mossy-green skin, pointed ears, wings like dried leaves, and a cap made from a chestnut husk, flies toward the lighthouse as the sun sits low on the horizon. The scene is rendered in Arthur Rackham style with detailed pen work, watercolor washes, and decorative borders framing the enchanted scene.
coverPrompt.alt: A fairy flying toward a lighthouse at sunset

questions.question: Who tells this story?
questions.explanation: The story is told by the lighthouse lens, which has watched over the waters for one hundred years.

questions.question: What problem does Thistle need to solve?
questions.explanation: Thistle must fix the failing lighthouse light before sunset to help the boats find their way home safely.

questions.question: How many steps are there in the lighthouse?
questions.explanation: The story tells us there are one hundred and twelve iron steps from top to bottom.

questions.question: What does Thistle use to move the heavy weight?
questions.explanation: Thistle uses the keeper's boat rope and the pulley system to move the weight that powers the lighthouse mechanism.

questions.question: How many fishing boats see the lighthouse beam?
questions.explanation: The story says three fishing boats see the beacon and turn safely toward shore.
